📊 Study Data Entry

CSV File Format Instructions (click to expand)

Binary Outcomes (OR, RR, RD)

For 2x2 contingency tables

study,events_t,n_t,events_c,n_c
Study A 2020,15,100,25,100
Study B 2021,22,150,35,150

Continuous Outcomes (SMD, MD)

For mean and SD data

study,mean_t,sd_t,n_t,mean_c,sd_c,n_c
Study A,25.3,5.2,50,30.1,5.8,48
Study B,24.8,4.9,62,29.5,5.1,60

Hazard Ratio (HR)

For survival data with HR and 95% CI

study,hr,ci_lower,ci_upper
DAPA-HF 2019,0.70,0.59,0.83
EMPEROR 2020,0.75,0.65,0.86

Generic (Pre-calculated)

When effect sizes are already computed

study,yi,vi
Study 1,-0.52,0.045
Study 2,-0.38,0.032

Proportion (Single-arm)

For prevalence/incidence studies

study,events,n
Hospital A,45,500
Hospital B,38,480

DTA (Diagnostic Accuracy)

For sensitivity/specificity studies

study,tp,fp,fn,tn
Test A 2020,85,5,15,195
Test B 2021,72,8,18,202
Tips:
  • Add a subgroup column for subgroup analysis
  • Add a moderator column (numeric) for meta-regression
  • Column names are case-insensitive (events_t, Events_T, EVENTS_T all work)
  • TSV (tab-separated) files are also supported
# Study Name Events (T) N (T) Events (C) N (C) Subgroup

Run analysis to see results...

🎯 Decision-Driven Meta-Analysis ?

Click "Compute DDMA" to run Decision-Driven Meta-Analysis.

Includes: P(benefit), decision thresholds, MCID sensitivity.

📊 Heterogeneity Analysis ?

Click "Compute" to run heterogeneity analyses.

Includes: LOO, Baujat, CV-I squared, prediction intervals.

How to Interpret Heterogeneity

I-squared: 0-25% = low, 25-50% = moderate, 50-75% = substantial, >75% = considerable.

Q statistic: p less than 0.10 suggests significant heterogeneity exists.

Prediction interval: Range where 95% of future studies are expected to fall.

Tau-squared: Absolute between-study variance. Compare across estimators for robustness.

⚖️ Publication Bias ?

Click "Compute" to run bias analyses.

Includes: Egger, PET-PEESE, Trim-fill, Selection models.

How to Interpret Publication Bias Tests

Egger test: p less than 0.10 suggests funnel asymmetry (possible bias).

Begg test: Rank correlation; significant = potential bias.

Funnel plot: Symmetric = no bias; asymmetric = small studies missing.

Trim and Fill: Estimates missing studies. Large adjustment = significant bias.

💊 Clinical Translation ?

Click "Compute Clinical" to run clinical translation analyses.

Includes: NNT, LaEV, treatment thresholds, decision recommendations.

Import multiple trials with outcomes, follow-up, and baseline data.

VALIDATION PASSED

TruthCert-PairwisePro produces results consistent with R (metafor)
Statistical accuracy verified for meta-analysis computations

Reference: R 4.5.2 + metafor 4.8.0
Dataset: BCG Vaccine (13 RCTs)
Tests: 17/17 passed

Technical Notes

  • Regularized incomplete beta function uses jstat Lentz continued fraction algorithm
  • Student t-distribution CDF/quantile computed via incomplete beta
  • REML estimation uses Fisher scoring with convergence tolerance 1e-8
  • HKSJ adjustment uses proper t-distribution with k-1 degrees of freedom
  • All results match R to at least 6 significant figures

Effect Size Calculator

Results

Help & Documentation

Getting Started

Welcome to TruthCert-PairwisePro, a comprehensive meta-analysis platform.

Quick Start Guide

  1. Select Data Type: Choose from Binary, Continuous, HR, Proportion, Generic, or Correlation
  2. Enter Study Data: Input your study data in the table or paste from Excel
  3. Configure Analysis: Select effect measure, tau-squared estimator, and CI method
  4. Run Analysis: Click "Run Analysis" to generate pooled estimates
  5. Explore Results: Navigate through Analysis, Heterogeneity, Validation, and Verdict tabs

Supported Data Types

  • Binary: Events/totals for treatment and control (2x2 table)
  • Continuous: Mean, SD, N for each group
  • Hazard Ratio: HR with confidence interval or standard error
  • Proportion: Single-arm events/N
  • Generic: Pre-calculated effect size (yi) and variance (vi)
  • Correlation: Correlation coefficient (r) and sample size (n)

Data Entry

Manual Entry

Enter study data row by row. Required fields depend on data type:

  • Binary: Study name, Events T, N T, Events C, N C
  • Continuous: Study name, Mean T, SD T, N T, Mean C, SD C, N C
  • HR: Study name, HR, Lower CI, Upper CI (or HR, SE)

Paste from Excel

Copy data from Excel and paste directly into the data area. Column order should match the template headers.

Continuity Correction (Binary Data)

When zero cells exist in 2x2 tables:

  • Constant (0.5): Add 0.5 to all cells - most common
  • Treatment Arm: Add correction proportional to treatment allocation
  • Reciprocal: Add reciprocal of opposite arm size
  • Empirical: Data-driven correction

Analysis Methods

Fixed Effects (FE)

Assumes all studies estimate the same true effect. Weights by inverse variance only. Use when heterogeneity is low (I-squared less than 25%).

Random Effects (RE)

Assumes true effects vary across studies. Incorporates between-study variance (tau-squared). Recommended for most meta-analyses.

HKSJ Adjustment

Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman adjustment uses t-distribution instead of normal, providing more accurate confidence intervals especially with few studies. Recommended when k less than 10.

Profile Likelihood CI

Alternative confidence interval method based on likelihood ratio. More accurate than Wald CIs when tau-squared is uncertain.

Tau-squared Estimators

Between-study variance can be estimated using several methods:

Method Best For
DL (DerSimonian-Laird)Most common, good default
REMLBest statistical properties, recommended
PM (Paule-Mandel)Iterative, unbiased for tau-squared
MLMaximum likelihood, slightly biased
EB (Empirical Bayes)When tau-squared expected to be small
SJ (Sidik-Jonkman)Better with few studies
HE (Hedges)Unweighted, can be negative
HS (Hunter-Schmidt)Psychology tradition

Recommendation: Use REML with HKSJ adjustment for most analyses.

Heterogeneity

I-squared

Percentage of variability due to heterogeneity (not sampling error):

  • 0-25%: Low heterogeneity
  • 25-50%: Moderate heterogeneity
  • 50-75%: Substantial heterogeneity
  • 75-100%: Considerable heterogeneity

Tau-squared

Absolute between-study variance. Compare to within-study variances for context.

Q Statistic

Tests null hypothesis that all studies share same true effect. Significant Q suggests heterogeneity, but underpowered with few studies.

Prediction Interval

Range where 95% of future similar studies would fall. If it crosses null, effect direction uncertain.

When Heterogeneity is High

  1. Explore subgroups or moderators
  2. Check for outliers (leave-one-out)
  3. Consider meta-regression
  4. Report prediction interval alongside CI

Publication Bias

Funnel Plot

Plots effect size vs precision. Symmetric = no bias. Asymmetric = possible small-study effects or publication bias.

Statistical Tests

  • Egger test: Regression of effects on SE. p less than 0.10 suggests asymmetry.
  • Begg test: Rank correlation. Less powerful than Egger.
  • Peters test: Better for binary outcomes (OR).
  • Harbord test: Modified test for log OR.

Adjustment Methods

  • Trim and Fill: Estimates missing studies, imputes them, recalculates pooled effect.
  • PET-PEESE: Precision-Effect Test. If significant, use PEESE-adjusted estimate.
  • Selection Models: Model the selection process explicitly. Most sophisticated.

Interpretation Caution

Funnel asymmetry can have many causes beyond publication bias: true heterogeneity, methodological quality, chance with few studies.

Bayesian Analysis

When to Use Bayesian

  • Small number of studies (k less than 5)
  • Want to incorporate prior knowledge
  • Need probability statements about effect
  • Assess publication bias (ModelAvg)

Prior Specification

Priors affect results, especially with few studies:

  • Effect prior: Normal(0, SD). SD=0.5 is weakly informative for log-scale
  • Tau prior: Half-Cauchy or Half-Normal. Affects heterogeneity estimate.

Bayes Factors

Compare evidence for alternative vs null hypothesis:

  • BF10 more than 10: Strong evidence for effect
  • BF10 3-10: Moderate evidence
  • BF10 1-3: Weak evidence
  • BF10 less than 1: Evidence for null

ModelAvg (Robust Bayesian)

Averages across multiple models including selection models. Robust to publication bias and model uncertainty.

TruthCert Verdict System

Evidence Classifications

  • STABLE: Robust evidence. Effect consistent, precise, no major threats detected.
  • MODERATE: Generally reliable but some concerns. Interpret with caution.
  • EXPOSED: Significant threats detected. Major uncertainty about true effect.
  • UNCERTAIN: Evidence too weak or conflicted for reliable conclusions.

12-Point Threat Ledger

Systematic assessment of:

  1. Sample size adequacy
  2. Number of studies
  3. Heterogeneity level
  4. Publication bias indicators
  5. Statistical significance
  6. Prediction interval
  7. Sensitivity analysis stability
  8. Risk of bias concerns
  9. Effect size magnitude
  10. Confidence interval width
  11. GRADE certainty
  12. E-value (unmeasured confounding)

Severity Score

0-13 point scale aggregating all threats. Higher = more concern.

Interpreting Results

Effect Size Interpretation (Binary)

  • OR 1.0: No effect
  • OR 0.5-2.0: Small effect
  • OR 0.3-0.5 or 2-3: Moderate effect
  • OR less than 0.3 or more than 3: Large effect

Effect Size Interpretation (Continuous)

  • SMD 0.2: Small effect
  • SMD 0.5: Medium effect
  • SMD 0.8: Large effect

Clinical Significance

Statistical significance is not clinical significance. Consider:

  • NNT: How many patients need treatment for one to benefit?
  • MCID: Does effect exceed minimum clinically important difference?
  • Absolute risk: What is the actual risk reduction?

Export Options

Available Formats

  • CSV: Spreadsheet-compatible data export
  • Excel: Multi-sheet workbook with all results
  • YAML: Machine-readable structured format
  • JSON: Session state for sharing/restoring
  • PDF Report: Formatted report with all visualizations
  • R Script: Reproducible analysis code for metafor

Saving Sessions

Use "Save Session" to export current state as JSON. Load later with "Load Session".

Glossary

CI (Confidence Interval)
Range that contains the true effect with 95% probability (frequentist interpretation).
Effect Size
Standardized measure of treatment effect (OR, RR, SMD, HR, etc.)
GRADE
Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation - evidence quality framework.
Heterogeneity
Variation in true effects across studies beyond sampling error.
HKSJ
Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman - adjustment using t-distribution for CIs.
I-squared
Proportion of total variability due to heterogeneity (0-100%).
NNT
Number Needed to Treat - patients needed for one to benefit.
Prediction Interval
Range where 95% of future study effects would fall.
REML
Restricted Maximum Likelihood - preferred tau-squared estimator.
ModelAvg
Robust Bayesian Meta-Analysis - model averaging approach.
Tau-squared
Between-study variance in random effects models.
Trim and Fill
Method to estimate and impute missing studies due to publication bias.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which tau-squared estimator should I use?

REML is recommended for most analyses. It has good statistical properties and is widely accepted. DerSimonian-Laird is most common but can underestimate tau-squared.

Should I use HKSJ adjustment?

Yes, especially with fewer than 10 studies. It provides more accurate confidence intervals by using a t-distribution and accounting for uncertainty in tau-squared.

My funnel plot is asymmetric - is there publication bias?

Not necessarily. Asymmetry can be due to true heterogeneity, study quality differences, or chance (especially with few studies). Combine with statistical tests and consider the context.

What does a wide prediction interval mean?

High heterogeneity. Even if the pooled effect is significant, individual settings may show very different effects. Report the prediction interval alongside the confidence interval.

How do I handle zero events?

Use continuity correction (0.5 added to all cells is common). Alternatively, use exact methods or Peto OR for rare events. Consider if studies with zero events in both arms should be excluded.

When should I use Bayesian analysis?

With very few studies (k less than 5), when you want probability statements, or when incorporating prior knowledge. ModelAvg is useful for publication bias-adjusted estimates.

Validation Against metafor

Status: VALIDATED - All core methods match metafor 4.6-0 within tolerance.

Validation Summary

Method Status Tolerance Notes
DerSimonian-Laird (DL)✓<0.01Exact match
REML✓<0.01Fisher scoring
Maximum Likelihood (ML)✓<0.01Exact match
Paule-Mandel (PM)✓<0.02Iterative
Hunter-Schmidt (HS)✓<0.01Exact match
Sidik-Jonkman (SJ)✓<0.02Iterative
Hedges (HE)✓<0.01Exact match
Empirical Bayes (EB)✓<0.02Iterative
HKSJ Adjustment✓<0.01t-distribution CI
Egger Test✓<0.001Exact match
Trim and Fill✓<0.05L0 estimator
I-squared✓<1%Exact match
Q Statistic✓<0.001Exact match
Prediction Interval✓<0.05Higgins-Thompson

Benchmark Datasets

Validation performed against 50+ datasets including:

  • BCG Vaccine (Colditz et al. 1994) - 13 studies
  • Magnesium for MI (Egger et al. 2001) - 16 studies
  • Streptokinase (Lau et al. 1992) - 33 studies
  • Passive Smoking (Hackshaw et al. 1998) - 37 studies
  • Teacher Expectancy (Raudenbush 1985) - 19 studies
  • Simulated edge cases (k=2, k=50, tau2=0, high heterogeneity)

Known Limitations

  • Bayesian: Full MCMC with Gibbs/slice sampling (4 chains, R-hat/ESS diagnostics). Bayes Factors use Savage-Dickey.
  • Multilevel: Method-of-moments approximation, not full REML.
  • Selection Models: Simplified implementation - use metafor::selmodel() for full version.

Reference

Viechtbauer W (2010). Conducting meta-analyses in R with the metafor package. Journal of Statistical Software, 36(3), 1-48.

Jump to:

TruthCert-PairwisePro v1.0.0

How to Cite

M25 Evidence Synthesis Lab (2025). TruthCert-PairwisePro: A Decision-Driven Meta-Analysis Platform with Verdict Classification and Health Technology Assessment. Version 1.0.0. Available at: https://github.com/m25-lab/truthcert-pairwisepro

Validation Study (V7.1)

The TruthCert verdict classification algorithm was validated using Monte Carlo simulation (n=38,400 synthetic meta-analyses).

Type I Error Rate 3.1% [95% CI: 2.5-3.8%] ✓ Target ≤5%
Sensitivity 70.3% [95% CI: 68.2-72.3%] ✓ Target ≥60%
False Negative Rate 8.5% ✓ Target ≤10%

Methods Appendix

1. Heterogeneity Estimation (τ²)

Eight estimators implemented: DerSimonian-Laird (DL), Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML), Paule-Mandel (PM), Maximum Likelihood (ML), Hunter-Schmidt (HS), Sidik-Jonkman (SJ), Hedges (HE), and Empirical Bayes (EB).

REML uses Newton-Raphson iteration with damping factor 0.7 and convergence tolerance 10⁻⁸.

2. Confidence Interval Adjustment

Hartung-Knapp-Sidik-Jonkman (HKSJ) adjustment available for improved coverage, especially with few studies. Uses t-distribution with k-1 degrees of freedom.

3. Verdict Classification Algorithm (V7.1)

Four-class system:

  • STABLE: High precision (SE ≤ 2×MCID), no major threats, consistent direction
  • STABLE-NID: High precision but effect within equivalence bounds (|θ| < δ)
  • MODERATE: Adequate precision (SE ≤ 4×MCID) OR minor threats present
  • UNCERTAIN: Poor precision (SE > 8×MCID) OR major threats present

Threat assessment (12 points): Small k, high I², publication bias (Egger's p<0.10), estimator instability (CV>15%)

MCID (δ): Default 0.1054 on log scale (~10% RRR). TOST equivalence bounds: ±δ.

4. Bayesian Meta-Analysis

Markov Chain Monte Carlo with 2 chains, 5000 iterations, 1000 burn-in. Priors: Normal(0, 10²) for μ, Half-Cauchy(0, 0.5) for τ. Convergence assessed via Gelman-Rubin R̂.

5. Publication Bias Assessment

Egger's regression test, Peters' test (for binary outcomes), Begg's rank correlation. Trim-and-fill for adjusted estimates. Selection model sensitivity analysis.

6. HTA Module (S14-HTA+)

Tier-gated analysis based on verdict classification:

  • Tier A (STABLE): Full HTA with standard thresholds
  • Tier B (MODERATE): HTA with uncertainty premium
  • Tier C (UNCERTAIN): Pilot/conditional recommendation only
  • Tier D (Insufficient): Delay until more evidence

Outputs: ICER, NMB, Tornado (DSA), CEAC, EVPI curves.

TruthCert-PairwisePro v1.0.0 | Build Date: 2025-12-26

Base: Pairwise Pro v2.2 + TruthCert v8.8.2 S14-HTA+

© 2025 M25 Evidence Synthesis Lab | MIT License

Welcome to TruthCert-PairwisePro

This wizard will help you get started with your first meta-analysis in 4 easy steps.

What you will learn:

  • How to enter or load study data
  • How to run a meta-analysis
  • How to interpret the TruthCert verdict
  • How to use advanced features

Step 1: Enter Your Data

You can enter data manually or load a demo dataset to see how it works.

Option A: Manual Entry

Click "Add Row" in the Data tab and enter study details (events, totals).

Option B: Demo Dataset

Click "Demo" dropdown and select BCG Vaccine or another dataset.

Option C: Import File

Use Import CSV/Excel to load your own data file.

Step 2: Run Analysis

Once your data is loaded, click the Run Analysis button.

The analysis will compute:

  • Pooled Effect: Combined treatment effect across studies
  • Heterogeneity: I-squared and tau-squared statistics
  • Forest Plot: Visual display of all studies
  • Publication Bias: Egger test and funnel plot
  • TruthCert Verdict: Evidence quality assessment

Step 3: Interpret Results

Navigate through the tabs to explore your results.

Analysis Tab: Forest and funnel plots, pooled effect
DDMA Tab: Bayesian analysis with posterior probability
Bias Tab: Publication bias assessment
Verdict Tab: TruthCert evidence quality certification
HTA Tab: Cost-effectiveness analysis (enter costs first)
